result:
The cursor blinks like a heartbeat that’s forgotten its own rhythm. One thousand words—sounds like a fence, but I’ve seen horizons built from fewer syllables, whole planets spun from a single vowel. I’ll start here, in the hush between your inhale and the next sentence, where the air tastes of copper pennies and thunderstorm static.

I once met a woman who collected extinct sounds. She kept them in mason jars lined along her windowsill, labeled in fountain-pen ink: dial-up modem, VHS rewind, the click of a cigarette lighter on a 1984 Buick. At dusk she unscrewed the metal lids and let the ghosts out, one by one, so her apartment sounded like a museum of almost-remembered futures. She told me the hardest sound to trap was the noise her mother’s coat made when it brushed against her cheek in the dark of a movie theater in 1993—part wool, part perfume, part heartbeat. Microphones only hear surfaces; memory records density. She died last winter. The landlord cleared the jars. Now some other tenant microwaves ramen beneath an empty sill, unaware the air still shivers with obsolete frequencies.

That’s the trick of time: it doesn’t pass, it accumulates, like dryer lint in the seams of the universe. We think we leave places, but really we shed them—skin flakes of cafés, eyelash wishes on subway poles—until the city becomes a composite portrait we wouldn’t recognize if we met ourselves coming the other way. I walked past my childhood home last week. The new owners painted it eggplant, ripped out the maple that used to impersonate a spaceship. Yet the crack in the sidewalk shaped like the state of Ohio remains, keeping its quiet census of chalk and roller-skate scars. I stood on it and felt the ledger balance: thirty-seven years of weight traded for thirty-seven years of memory, exact change only.

Language is heavier. My grandfather spoke a dialect of silence peppered with nouns. He could say “pass the wrench” in four syllables and “I love you” in none. When the stroke subtracted his sentences, we found his toolbox full of folded paper cranes, each wing inked with coordinates: the field where he lost his brother’s kite, the latitude of the first bombs, the longitude of the hospital where I was born while he waited in the hallway, hat in hand, whispering to god in a language heaven had never bothered to learn. After the funeral I unfolded every bird, strung their numbers along a cord of fishing line, and hung them across my bedroom like Christmas lights. On sleepless nights I read the glowing decimals like prayers until the room spins into globe, and I can pinpoint exactly where grief intersects with the international date line.

We misunderstand death as a border; it’s more like a subway grating—every day we walk over the roaring, coins slipping through the slats, and someday the metal gives. The difference is acoustics. The living hear Train; the dead hear Departure, same vibration, alternate mix. My friend Joi, jazz drummer, claimed she could play the difference on the ride cymbal. She demonstrated at 2:17 a.m. in a basement club that smelled of bleach and clove cigarettes. The bartender wept without knowing why. Later, crossing the street, Joi was collected by a driver who never saw her shadow. At the service I kept waiting for the rim-shot punchline, but grief doesn’t observe comedic timing. Now, whenever I hear brakes hiss, I add a sixteenth-note rest in her honor, a pocket of quiet the size of a heartbeat, impossible to notate yet unmistakably felt.

Sometimes I think we are all just footnotes in each other’s dissertations on loss. The librarian who stamps due dates onto my borrowed griefs has a tattoo of a phoenix that never rises; it circles its own ashes like a dog unwilling to leave the yard where the gate was left open. She says the trick is not resurrection but re-shelving—put the sorrow in the right Dewey decimal neighborhood so someone else can find it when needed. I asked for the call number of the woman who collects sounds. She handed me a blank index card, smiled like a library closing for the night, and whispered, “Try the stacks that haven’t been built yet.”

I walked home along the river, where the water practices its handwriting against the seawall: same sentence for millennia, still can’t get the loops right. A goose honked once, a baritone sax note, and I realized cities are just slow-motion improvisations—every car horn an accidental, every siren a blue-note bend. The skyline is chord progression in steel and glass; wait long enough and even the bricks resolve. I tried to hum along but my mouth filled with wind, so I swallowed it and felt the weather settle in my stomach like a prophecy: tomorrow, scattered wishes, probability of regret sixty percent, higher near the coast.

Here is the secret they never tell you: quantity is quality wearing a fake mustache. One thousand words equals roughly four pages, but also one entire childhood if you choose verbs wisely. You can trade them like currency: ten adjectives for one honest apology, three metaphors for a parent’s forgiveness, a subordinate clause for the name of the color your eyes turned when you first understood forever was retractable. The exchange rate fluctuates nightly; check the wall above your pillow where the dreams post their closing bell. This morning: pronouns up, articles down, interjections holding steady. Analysts blame nostalgia for the surge.

I keep a spare tomorrow in my wallet, folded behind the photo ID that insists I’m still twenty-nine and capable of running two miles without stopping to google the symptoms of imminent cardiac event. Sometimes I slip it to a stranger on the train when their face buckles under the weight of invisible rainfall. They look at me suspicious, as if kindness were counterfeit, but occasionally they spend it right there on the red line: let someone else exit first, pick up the dropped glove, meet the eyes of the kid drawing unicorns on the fogged window. Compound interest, the universe accruing tiny currencies of astonishment until one day the purse strings of the sky unzip and down drifts enough mercy to pay off everybody’s overdue fines.

Word count climbs like ivy, unaware of the wall’s intent. I could pivot to manifesto, recipe, love letter, ransom note—each genre a different hat on the same head. But the head keeps changing, jawline softening, hair receding like glacial tidelines, ears elongating to accommodate the echo of everything they refuse to unhear. Mirrors are just outdated search engines; they return images instead of answers. When I type my name into the cloud, autocorrect suggests “mortal,” then “moral,” then “mortal” again, as if the algorithm is arguing with itself about the spelling of salvation.

Let’s end here: halfway between the promised fence and the horizon that never signed the contract. Eight hundred plus spent; a pocketful left to jingle. I could tell you the sound coins make falling into a wishing well is identical to the sound a pulse makes when it decides to stay on this side of the skin for one more day, but you already know that. You knew before I began. The rest is just syllables rehearsing for exile, letters lining up to be shot at dawn by the firing squad of white space. Fire when ready.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
